_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Operators
summary: Calculate values, compare them, and combine conditions.
refresher: C++ uses familiar arithmetic operators such as `+`, `-`, `*`, and `/`. Comparisons such as `>=` produce true or false. Use `&&` for and, `||` for or, `!` for not, and `==` to compare equality.

blocks.body: Operators perform work with values. Arithmetic operators calculate, comparison operators ask a true-or-false question, and logical operators join those questions. Parentheses make a longer expression easier to follow.

The types on both sides affect the result. Two integers divided together produce an integer, so `7 / 2` gives `3`. The logical operators `&&` and `||` stop as soon as the final answer is known, which can prevent the expression on the right from running.

blocks.title: Logical words look different in Python
blocks.body: Python spells these operators `and`, `or`, and `not`. C++ writes the same ideas as `&&`, `||`, and `!`. The meaning is the same even though the symbols differ.

blocks.caption: The first calculation uses two integers and drops the fraction. Writing 7.0 makes the second calculation use decimal arithmetic.
Convert before the operation that would lose information.

blocks.body: If `total` is seven and `count` is two, integer division produces three before assignment happens. Storing that result in a double changes it to `3.0`, but the missing half is already gone. Convert before dividing.

blocks.title: Comparison uses two equals signs
blocks.body: Use `==` to ask whether two values are equal. A single `=` stores a new value. Mixing them up inside a condition can change a variable when you only meant to compare it.

blocks.why: Multiplication happens first, leaving ten minus six.
The remainder after two groups of four is one.
